elements.sent-2411-001: Medical image segmentation is crucial in robotic surgeries, disease diagnosis, and treatment plans.
elements.sent-2411-002: This research presents an innovative methodology that combines Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN) with an adapted Mamba layer for medical image segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-003: The proposed KAN-Mamba FusionNet framework improves image segmentation by integrating attention-driven mechanisms with convolutional parallel training and autoregressive deployment, while preserving interpretability, in contrast to the state-of-the-art techniques that depend exclusively on Mamba for ailment localization and accurate diagnosis.
elements.sent-2411-004: We evaluated our proposed KAN-Mamba FusionNet model on three distinct medical image segmentation datasets, BUSI, Kvasir-Seg and GlaS.
elements.sent-2411-005: The results indicated that the KAN-Mamba FusionNet consistently yields better IoU and F1 scores in comparison to the state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-006: Further, we offer insights into the model's behavior via ablation studies, examining the effects of various components and assessing their contributions to the overall performance of the proposed model.
elements.sent-2411-007: The findings illustrate the strength and effectiveness of this methodology for dependable medical image segmentation, providing a unique approach to address intricate visual data issues in healthcare.
elements.sent-2411-008: Medical image segmentation can help with the accurate localization of anatomical features, leading to the timely detection of abnormalities and patient treatment.
elements.sent-2411-009: Recent research into developing advanced deep learning and computer vision-based approaches has provided a gateway to address the challenges faced in the localization of diseased organs \cite{zhang2021medical, kevric2022deep, church2019deep, ronneberger2015unet .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Reliable image segmentation techniques are vital for helping medical experts make informed decisions and improve diagnosis outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-011: This can potentially increase the life expectancy of the patients.
elements.sent-2411-012: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are popularly used to mark individual pixels for segmentation tasks to identify tumor locations, organs, and relevant anatomical features.
elements.sent-2411-013: Classical CNN-based approaches like UNet models \cite{ronneberger2015unet are based on encoder and decoder architecture for image segmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The UNet variations, UNet \cite{ronneberger2015unet , UNet++ \cite{zhou2018unetplusplus , and UNet3+ \cite{huang2020unet3plus use skip connections to combine the related low-level detailed feature maps from the encoder and the high-level semantic feature maps from the decoder.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Since these models use hierarchical techniques to extract features from the input image, they fail to get global contextual dependencies, which are essential to segment varied-sized anatomical parts from the medical image.
elements.sent-2411-016: Unlike CNNs, ViT i.e. vision transformers \cite{dosovitskiy2020image use attention mechanism to capture the global contextual dependencies and can accept varied input sizes, making them better suitable for the medical image segmentation task \cite{chen2021transunet .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, transformers suffer from large model sizes, high memory, and computational requirements due to long-run feature extractions.
elements.sent-2411-018: In order to overcome the challenges of higher computational complexity of transformers, state space models (SSM) using linear RNNs (Recurrent Neural Networks) are proposed as Mamba architecture \cite{gu2023mamba .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Different variants of Mamba architecture, such as U-Mamba \cite{lu2024umamba , Vision Mamba \cite{wang2024visionmamba , and SegMamba \cite{wang2024segmamba were introduced to improve the overall efficiency of the medical image segmentation by employing selective state representations that dynamically adjust based on the input data.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: These models were able to achieve the time complexity of $O(nlog(n))$ which is much lower than the transformers.
elements.sent-2411-021: The Mamba-based models use the convolution layers which are similar to Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs).
elements.sent-2411-022: In past, the MLP layers have been replaced with KAN Architecture \cite{liu2024kan to capture the nonlinear intricacies in the images.}
elements.sent-2411-023: For instance, the boundaries between the features of images may not capture a linear relation, leading to a loss of some important details from the medical images which can potentially result in incorrect disease diagnosis.
elements.sent-2411-024: To address this issue the U-KAN based approach was proposed for medical image segmentation \cite{li2024ukan by learning the activation functions to capture the non-linear features.}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, the approach utilized in KAN-based architecture has a disadvantage over Mamba because of its inability to capture self-attentive based learning over intricate spatial features from the images \cite{woo2018cbam .}
elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper, we propose KAN-Mamba FusionNet model for medical image segmentation to address the challenges posed by the currently available techniques.
elements.sent-2411-027: The proposed architecture incorporates KAN in Mamba layer and enables the integration of non-linear transformations to improve the representation of complex medical imaging data.
elements.sent-2411-028: Here, we leverage the synergy of KAN and Mamba-based architectures to overcome the limitations of conventional CNNs and Transformers, particularly in their ability to capture long-range dependencies and handle image segmentation datasets efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-029: Moreover, standard activation functions such as ReLU, sigmoid, and tanh used in the MLPs, Mamba and KAN models possess distinct advantages and drawbacks.
elements.sent-2411-030: For example, ReLU provides efficiency and sparsity but may encounter the "dying ReLU" issue, whereas sigmoid and tanh are suitable for probability modeling but are susceptible to vanishing gradients.
elements.sent-2411-031: To tackle these issues, we present the Bag-of-Activation (BoA) Functions in our proposed approach, which integrates many activation functions via a weighted sum with adjustable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-032: This approach enables neural networks to flexibly leverage the benefits of various activation functions, hence augmenting their capacity to represent intricate data patterns and enhancing overall efficacy.
elements.sent-2411-033: Here is the summary of our contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-034: We propose a novel neural network architecture, KAN-Mamba FusionNet, that incorporates Mamba block in the U-KAN model to introduce self-attention layers for complex contextual understanding from the input data for improved localization.
elements.sent-2411-035: We additionally introduce an innovative enhancement by adding another KAN layer within the Mamba architecture to further integrate learning over any non-linear intricacies in the input.
elements.sent-2411-036: Further, we introduce the support of Bag of Activation (BoA) functions in the Mamba block, which dynamically combines multiple activation functions to provide a more robust representation of features.
elements.sent-2411-037: We evaluated our proposed architecture on medical image datasets, BUSI \cite{al2020busi , GlaS \cite{valanarasu2021kiu and Kvasir-Seg \cite{jha2020kvasir and provided ablation study in the end.}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Medical image segmentation is crucial for enabling accurate diagnosis and treatment planning.
elements.sent-2411-039: Architectures like U-Net, characterized by its encoder-decoder structure, have shown significant success in this domain \cite{ronneberger2015unet .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Extensions such as Unet++ \cite{zhou2018unetplusplus and CE-Net \cite{gu2019cenet incorporate sophisticated modules for multi-scale feature fusion and contextual encoding to improve segmentation accuracy.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: In addition to convolution-based approaches, transformer models \cite{vaswani2017attention like Vision Transformer \cite{dosovitskiy2021image , Medical Transformer \cite{valanarasu2021medical , and TransUNet \cite{chen2021transunet have been applied, using attention mechanisms to capture long-range dependencies.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Methods like 3D segmentation \cite{kamnitsas2017efficient and multi-dimensional gated recurrent units \cite{andermatt2016multi have shown promise in tasks requiring volumetric understanding.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Recently, Mamba-based architectures have emerged, utilizing state-space models (SSMs) for fast linear-time inference and cross-scan modules \cite{gu2023mamba,liu2024vmamba .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Their applications, especially on biomedical image segmentation datasets, highlight their growing importance in medical imaging.
elements.sent-2411-045: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) offer an innovative alternative to traditional MLPs for medical segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-046: Based on the Kolmogorov-Arnold theorem, KANs efficiently approximate multivariate continuous functions using univariate transformations, enabling the capture of non-linearities in high-dimensional data \cite{huang2014kan .}
elements.sent-2411-047: KANs are distinguished by their interpretability, robustness, and efficiency, making them suitable for applications requiring precision, such as medical imaging.
elements.sent-2411-048: While their application in medical segmentation is nascent, models like U-KAN that employ learnable weights and nonlinear transformations have shown potential for extracting insights from various medical datasets.
elements.sent-2411-049: Mamba-based models are designed to address the limitations of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) in capturing long-range dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-050: Vision Mamba (Vim) and VMamba have demonstrated improved effectiveness in video understanding and remote sensing tasks by combining state-space models with attention mechanisms \cite{liu2024vmamba .}
elements.sent-2411-051: In medical imaging, these models have proven particularly efficient in segmentation, utilizing approaches such as transfer learning on biomedical image segmenation datasets.
elements.sent-2411-052: A comparative study of these architectures with CNNs and Vision Transformers (ViTs) has highlighted the trade-offs in terms of data requirements, interpretability, and performance.
elements.sent-2411-053: Biomedical image segmentations has seen several algorithmic improvements.
elements.sent-2411-054: Hybrid models such as the Hybrid Adaptive Attention Module (HAAM)-based U-Net \cite{chen2023aaunet and attention-guided U-Net \cite{punn2022attention have achieved high precision and accuracy, using attention mechanisms to enhance feature extraction.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Alternative designs like U2-MNet \cite{li2020u2mnet and SU-Next \cite{zhu2021sunext employ advancements such as residual inception depth-wise separable convolution and multilayer perceptron frameworks to improve segmentation speed and accuracy.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Models like the Feature Pyramid Non-Local Network \cite{tang2018fpnn and cascaded CNNs \cite{chen2019cascaded have improved performance metrics on datasets like Breast UltraSound Images (BUSI), achieving significant dice scores.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Techniques such as transfer learning and novel attention mechanisms are advancing image segmentation, with KANs and Mamba-based models offering viable alternatives to traditional CNN structures.
elements.sent-2411-058: In this paper, we propose a novel architecture, KAN-Mamba FusionNet, which leverages the non-linear intricacies captured by KAN as well as utilizes the capabilities of Mamba to capture the implicit spatial features from the input images.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{sec/KANMambacorrection.png} \caption{Overall Architectural Diagram. Note that each layer is connected with the next adjacent layer along with other explicit connections mentioned.} \label{fig:overall-image}
elements.sent-2411-059: The proposed KAN-Mamba FusionNet architecture integrates the attentive-driven mechanism with the robust KAN non-linear model \cite{huang2014kan .}
elements.sent-2411-060: The idea is to replace the convolution and batch normalization layers (which are similar to MLPs) used before the SSM model in the traditional Mamba \cite{gu2023mamba with the KAN block.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Figure \ref{fig:overall-image represents the overall architectural flow of our proposed model.}
elements.sent-2411-062: It utilizes the U-KAN architecture \cite{li2024ukan as a base.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The notations or color encoding done in the overall architecture diagram (Figure \ref{fig:overall-image ) represent the following:.}
elements.sent-2411-065: P1/P2: Represents the patch embedding layer.
elements.sent-2411-066: M1: Represents the modified Mamba-KAN block.
elements.sent-2411-068: D1/D2/D3/D4/D5: Represents the DepthWise Convolution ($DwConvB$) Block.
elements.sent-2411-070: O1: Represents Conv2D layer, yielding the final output.
elements.sent-2411-071: The proposed architectural flow is inspired by the KAN blocks used in the past in the replacement of the traditional MLP layer \cite{huang2014kan .}
elements.sent-2411-072: To capture the non-linear intricacies, KAN use learnable weights over edges.
elements.sent-2411-073: Similarly, in the past there has been usage of SSM and attentive layer to selectively process the input given and extract the relevant information \cite{liu2024vmamba .}
elements.sent-2411-074: Combining those, we propose to use a Mamba block with the KAN block.
elements.sent-2411-075: Further, since Mamba blocks consist of the convolution and batch norm layers (which is similar to the processing method of traditional MLP), we propose a change to replace those layers with a single KAN layer.
elements.sent-2411-076: Moreover, we propose an idea to use a variation in activation function by replacing a single activation function with a bag of activation functions, where the model learns the weights assigned to each of the functions.
elements.sent-2411-077: The implementation of the proposed architecture blocks is explained in detail as follows: Convolutional Block: The Convolution blocks C1/C2/C3, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:conv-image , consist of 2D convolution layers, batch normalization layers, a max pooling layer and ReLU activation functions.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Let us represent this block transformation as $ConvB(.)$ such that : \begin{equation}Z_{\text{out}} = ConvB(Z_{\text{in}})\end{equation} where $Z_{in}$ is the input sent to the convolution block.
elements.sent-2411-079: In a similar way, as depicted in Figure \ref{fig:wide-image , we have expressed depthwise convolution block, $DwConvB$ by replacing pool operation in $ConvB$ by interpolation operation: \begin{equation}Z_{\text{out}} = DwConvB(Z_{\text{in}})\end{equation} Mamba Block: The Mamba block is added after the first convolutional block, $ConvB C1$ as shown in Figure \ref{fig:overall-image .}}
elements.sent-2411-080: The input data passes through the linear projection layer while keeping the original input intact for the final linear combination.
elements.sent-2411-081: After the linear projection, the convolution kernel layer is applied, convolving over the 2D spatial dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-082: Further, it is processed via batch normalization 2D layer and a spatial attention layer.
elements.sent-2411-083: The step from convolution to batch normalization is organized into a series, forming a three-mini-block structure, with a spatial attention layer separating these blocks.
elements.sent-2411-084: The output is then passed through the activation function, which is further fed into a State Space Model (SSM) block.
elements.sent-2411-085: Additionally, a spatial attention layer is applied for the final processing.
elements.sent-2411-086: The attentive layer on the Convolutional Block Attention Module can be expressed as \cite{woo2018cbam : \begin{equation}M_s(F) = \sigma\left(f_{7 \times 7}\left(\left[AvgPool(F); MaxPool(F)\right]\right)\right)\end{equation} \begin{equation}= \sigma\left(f_{7 \times 7}\left(\left[F_{s\text{avg}}; F_{s\text{max}}\right]\right)\right)\end{equation} here, $\sigma$ represents the sigmoid function acting over a larger convolution function $f_{7 \times 7}$ having filter size of 7 × 7 to capture more spatial features.}
elements.sent-2411-087: After reshaping, this output is linearly combined with the input (forming a skip connection) and with the output of the activation function in which the same input was fed.
elements.sent-2411-088: Bag of Activation Functions: The single activation function, sulu, utilized in the Mamba block is substituted with the bag of activation functions.
elements.sent-2411-089: The model learns the weights assigned to the bag of activation functions to find the most effective function.
elements.sent-2411-090: The bag of activation function can be expressed as : \begin{equation}\Psi(Z) = \sum_{p=1}^P \alpha_{p} \, \psi_{p}(Z) \label{eq:boa}\end{equation} where $\alpha_{p}$ forms the learnable weight associated with activation function $\psi_{p}$.
elements.sent-2411-091: In our architecture, we have included following activation functions in the bag - ReLU, Tanh, Softplus, GELU and SiLU, keeping same initial weights.
elements.sent-2411-092: KAN in Mamba: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:mamba-kan-image , the architecture of this block primarily differs in the convolution layers used in Mamba before the SSM.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Those convolution layers are replaced with a KAN block \cite{li2024ukan .}
elements.sent-2411-094: The input data is first passed through a patch embedding layer and then processed with a single KAN layer.
elements.sent-2411-095: Further, the output is passed through an activation function, which is then fed into an SSM block.
elements.sent-2411-096: After spatial transformation, the results are processed by a spatial attention layer, followed by a linear projection layer.
elements.sent-2411-097: The resizing done in the projection layer helps to linearly combine the output with the input (from skip connection) and the output of the activation layer on the same input.
elements.sent-2411-098: The MLP having K layers can be expressed in following form \cite{li2024ukan : \begin{equation}\text{MLP}(Z) = \left( W_{K-1} \circ \sigma \circ W_{K-2} \circ \sigma \circ \cdots \circ W_1 \circ \sigma \circ W_0 \right) Z\end{equation} where $W_k$ with $k\in[1,K]$ represent the transformation matrix, $\sigma$ is the activation function and $Z$ is the input data.}
elements.sent-2411-099: In a similar way we can write KAN formulation \cite{li2024ukan : \begin{equation}\text{KAN}(Z) = (\Phi_{K-1} \circ \Phi_{K-2} \circ \cdots \circ \Phi_1 \circ \Phi_0) Z\end{equation} where $\Phi$ are expressed using learnable activation functions $\phi$ such that \begin{equation}\Phi = \{\phi_{q,p}\}, \quad p = 1, 2, \dots, n_{\text{in}}, \quad q = 1, 2, \dots, n_{\text{out}}\end{equation} The KAN layer consists of the KAN Linear transformation \cite{li2024ukan , followed by a depthwise convolution $DwConv$ layer (which consists of Conv2D Layer, followed by a batch normalization and a ReLU activation function).}}
elements.sent-2411-100: The basic unit of KAN layer can be represented as: \begin{equation}Z_{out} = (DwConv \circ KANLinear) Z_{in}\end{equation} Such transformation is repeated thrice to get the KAN layer.
elements.sent-2411-101: Therefore, output can be mathematically denoted as: \begin{equation}\Gamma_{out} = KANB(Z_{in})\end{equation} where $Z_{in}$ is the input passed through the KAN block.
elements.sent-2411-102: Now, the mathematical formulation of modified Mamba-KAN block is explained below in detail.
elements.sent-2411-103: The input $Z_{in}$ is first passed through a patch embedding layer. \begin{equation}Z_{\text{out}} = PatchEmbed(Z_{\text{in}})\end{equation} The $PatchEmbed(.)$ represents the similar tokenization step in Tokenized KAN phrase operation as proposed in Seg.
elements.sent-2411-105: Then this is fed into KAN block such that : \begin{equation}Z_{\text{out}}' = KANB(Z_{\text{out}})\end{equation} where the $KANB(.)$ operation as mentioned earlier represent the KAN block transformation.
elements.sent-2411-106: This is further processed by a layer of Bag of Activation (BoA) Functions such that \begin{equation}Z_{\text{out}}'' = \Psi(Z_{\text{out}}')\end{equation} Further, the output is passed through state space model (SSM) for transformation to get the following output: \begin{equation}Z_{\text{out}}''' = SSM(Z_{\text{out}}'')\end{equation} After this, an attentive layer is added, which results in the following: \begin{equation}Z_{\text{out}}'''' = M_s(Z_{\text{out}}''')\end{equation} where $M_s(.)$ represents the attentive function as explained above already.
elements.sent-2411-107: Finally, combination with the skip connection and a parallel layer of BoA Functions gives final output as \begin{equation}\Omega_{\text{out}}(Z_\text{in}) = Z_{\text{out}}'''' \oplus Z_{in} \oplus \Psi(Z_{\text{in}})\end{equation} Moreover, the loss for overall architecture is calculated using a combination of binary cross entropy (BCE) loss function and dice loss function, measuring the deviation from ground truth labels for entire set of N pixels. \begin{equation}\text{BCE}(\hat{z},z) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( z_i \cdot \log(\hat{z}_i) + (1 - z_i) \cdot \log(1 - \hat{z}_i) \right)\end{equation} \begin{equation}\text{DiceLoss}(\hat{z},z) = 1- \frac{2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} \hat{z}_i \cdot z_i + \text{c}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \hat{z}_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i + \text{c}}\end{equation} where $z_i$ and $\hat{z _i$ represent the pixel-wise label from actual and estimated measures respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-108: A small constant $c$ is included in dice loss to handle division by zero.

U-KAN \cite{li2024ukan} 59.23$\pm$1.87 73.79$\pm$1.48 75.85 $\pm$ 0.53 85.96 $\pm$ 0.35 84.75 $\pm$ 0.32 91.70 $\pm$ 0.20 \rowcolor[rgb]{0.678,0.847,0.902} KAN-Mamba FusionNet 62.41$\pm$0.65 76.41$\pm$0.56 76.95 $\pm$ 0.68 86.66 $\pm$ 0.43 84.92 $\pm$ 0.45 91.82 $\pm$ 0.27 \end{tabular} \caption{Performance comparison of various segmentation methods on BUSI, Kvasir-Seg and GlaS datasets.} \label{tab:comparisons}

U-KAN \cite{li2024ukan} 13.87 6.36 \rowcolor[rgb]{0.678,0.847,0.902} KAN-Mamba FusionNet 13.96 6.37 \end{tabular} \caption{Model complexity comparison of various methods.} \label{tab:complexity}
elements.figure-2411-008: \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \multirow{2}{*}{Blocks and/or Layers} \multicolumn{6}{c|}{Performance Metrics} IoU$\uparrow$ F1$\uparrow$ Accuracy$\uparrow$ AUC$\uparrow$ Precision$\uparrow$ Recall$\uparrow$ Mamba + MLP 58.81 73.27 92.53 91.96 74.43 72.58 Mamba + KAN 60.64 74.98 92.93 92.70 75.18 75.07 Mamba with BoA + KAN 61.35 75.40 93.26 92.78 76.68 73.48 \rowcolor[rgb]{0.678,0.847,0.902} KAN-Mamba FusionNet 62.41 76.41 93.42 93.33 77.76 75.41 \end{tabular} \caption{Ablation studies on the effect of introducing changes in the Mamba block on the model on BUSI dataset.} \label{tab:mamba}

elements.sent-2411-109: We evaluated our model on three distinct medical image segmentation datasets: Breast UltraSound Images (BUSI) \cite{al2020busi , Segmented Polyp Images \cite{jha2020kvasir and Gland Segmentation Images (GlaS) \cite{valanarasu2021kiu .}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Given the unique characteristics of each dataset, these evaluations offer a strong support for testing the effectiveness of our method.
elements.sent-2411-111: BUSI \cite{al2020busi : The dataset consists of medical images from ultrasound scans, along with their corresponding segmentation masks, used for identifying breast cancer-related tumors.}
elements.sent-2411-112: The entire set consists of 708 images, out of which 210, 437 and 133 represent the respective number of images for malignant, benign and normal breast cancer cases.
elements.sent-2411-113: We utilized the images representing breast cancer.
elements.sent-2411-114: The images were uniformly resized to 256 × 256 pixels.
elements.sent-2411-115: Kvasir-Seg \cite{jha2020kvasir : Kvasir-SEG dataset consists of 1000 gastrointestinal polyp images (polyps are precursors to colorectal cancer) and their corresponding segmentation masks, which were manually annotated by a medical doctor and subsequently verified by an experienced gastroenterologist.}
elements.sent-2411-116: All images were uniformly resized to 256 × 256 pixels.
elements.sent-2411-117: GlaS \cite{valanarasu2021kiu : The dataset consists of gland segmentation images and is associated with the Hospital Clinic in Barcelona, Spain.}
elements.sent-2411-118: For our study, we used 165 images from the dataset, all resized to 256 × 256 pixels.
elements.sent-2411-119: For all three datasets, we set the learning rate to 1e-4 and trained with the Adam optimizer, incorporating a cosine annealing learning rate scheduler with a minimum learning rate of 1e-5.
elements.sent-2411-120: The loss function used was a combination of binary cross-entropy and dice loss.
elements.sent-2411-121: Each dataset was split in a 4:1 ratio for training and validation, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-122: Training was conducted over 400 epochs, with basic data augmentations like random rotation and flipping, applied to the inputs.
elements.sent-2411-123: To evaluate the model's performance, we reported validation IoU (Intersection over Union) and F1 Score on all the datasets and compared with the state-of-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-124: The IoU measures the overlap between predicted and ground truth segments, while the F1 score provides a balanced measure of precision and recall.
elements.sent-2411-125: These metrics are crucial for understanding the model’s accuracy and reliability across various segmentation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-126: The reported results are averages from three independent runs.
elements.sent-2411-127: We also present computational cost metrics such as GFLOPs and the total number of model parameters, and compare these with state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-128: Additionally, we performed an ablation studies of our model, with the help of other useful parameters.
elements.sent-2411-129: Table \ref{tab:comparisons shows performance comparisons with other state-of-art methods on image segmentation on the three datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-130: We introduce comparisons to the five state-of-art methods, U-Net \cite{ronneberger2015unet , U-NeXt \cite{valanarasu2021medical , Rolling-UNet \cite{liu2024rolling , U-Mamba \cite{ma2024u , and Seg.. U-KAN \cite{li2024ukan .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: Our experimental results demonstrate that, across all datasets, our proposed method, KAN-Mamba FusionNet, consistently performs well in comparison to other state-of-the-art approaches.
elements.sent-2411-132: Our model performance, as demonstrated in terms of both metrics, IoU and F1 score, clearly indicates its robustness and versatility in handling diverse segmentation challenges.
elements.sent-2411-133: Further, as F1 score provides a balanced measure of precision and recall, in medical diagnosis, this model is very effective at identifying the actual positive cases, minimizing the number of false negatives (i.e., failing to detect cases where the condition is actually present), as such a failure in detection could be dangerous, leading to serious consequences for anyone.
elements.sent-2411-134: Additionally, as shown in Table \ref{tab:complexity we have calculated the GFLOPs and model parameters to emphasize that our model can be implemented with minimal trade-off in computational cost in contrast to previous methods.}
elements.sent-2411-135: We do a comprehensive evaluation of our proposed KAN-Mamba FusionNet under various settings, specifically to understand the effect of some added features in our model.
elements.sent-2411-136: Effect of Changes in Mamba Block: Table \ref{tab:mamba shows the results of the ablation study to validate the effect of modifications in the Mamba block.}
elements.sent-2411-137: As shown in Table \ref{tab:mamba , the Mamba + MLP combination represents the classical Mamba followed by an MLP layer.}
elements.sent-2411-138: However, the classical model underperforms, whereas replacing the MLP layer with KAN layers leads to improvements in IoU, F1, accuracy, AUC, precision as well as recall, as indicated by the validation metrics in the table.
elements.sent-2411-139: Furthermore, incorporating a bag of activation functions alongside in classical Mamba, and then replacing the MLP layer with KAN layers (as proposed in our model), further improves the listed performance parameters.
elements.sent-2411-140: Effect of Bag of Activation Functions: As shown in Table \ref{tab:ablation1 , the model initially performed sub-optimally when no additional activation function layer is added to the Mamba block, as proposed in our architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-141: However, when a single activation function (say, ReLU) is introduced, the model's performance improved significantly.
elements.sent-2411-142: Furthermore, by incorporating a composition of multiple activation functions, essentially a "Bag of Activation functions", the model's learning capability is further enhanced, leading to even better results.
elements.sent-2411-143: These results underscore the importance of adding a bag of activation functions, demonstrating that the diversity of activation functions plays a important role in enhancing the performance of the model.
elements.sent-2411-144: Different activation functions capture distinct aspects of data, each bringing their own strengths and weaknesses to a neural network.
elements.sent-2411-145: For instance, the Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) \cite{nair2010rectified is defined as: \begin{equation}\text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0, x)\end{equation} ReLU introduces sparsity by activating only for positive inputs, which can lead to faster convergence and reduced computational complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-146: However, ReLU can suffer from the ``dying ReLU'' problem, where neurons may become inactive and stop learning if they consistently output zero.
elements.sent-2411-147: On the other hand, the sigmoid function \cite{han1995influence , defined as: \begin{equation}\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}},\end{equation} is adept at modeling probabilities and is useful in scenarios requiring output values between 0 and 1.}
elements.sent-2411-148: Nonetheless, it is prone to vanishing gradients, which can hinder the training of deep networks.
elements.sent-2411-149: Similarly, the hyperbolic tangent (tanh) \cite{lecun1998efficient activation function: \begin{equation}\text{tanh}(x) = \frac{e^{x} - e^{-x}}{e^{x} + e^{-x}},\end{equation} captures symmetric relationships around zero, providing outputs in the range of -1 to 1, which can help in centering the data and improving convergence.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Yet, it also suffers from vanishing gradient issues.
elements.sent-2411-151: To address these limitations, we proposed a combining multiple activation functions into a Bag of Activation (BoA) Functions as defined in the equation \eqref{eq:boa .}
elements.sent-2411-152: This formulation allows the network to explore a richer function space, enhancing its ability to model diverse patterns in the data.
elements.sent-2411-153: By optimizing the weights associated with each activation function during training, the model can leverage the strengths of each activation function while mitigating their individual weaknesses.
elements.sent-2411-154: For example, the combination can dynamically balance the sparsity introduced by ReLU with the probabilistic modeling of sigmoid and the zero-centered outputs of tanh.
elements.sent-2411-155: This adaptive weighting is achieved through gradient-based optimization, ensuring that the network selects the most appropriate activation behavior for different tasks or data characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-156: Furthermore, different parts of the network might benefit from different activation behaviors, enabling more specialized and adaptive nonlinear transformations.
elements.sent-2411-157: By enabling the network to adaptively combine multiple activation functions, the Bag of Activation Functions approach offers a robust and versatile mechanism for enhancing neural network performance in diverse machine learning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-158: In this paper, we propose KAN-Mamba FusionNet architecture for medical image segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-159: It integrates Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN) with Mamba, and the bag of activation functions to reduce complexity, capture non-linearity in the input images, and adaptively select the efficient activation function to boost the model performance respectively.
elements.sent-2411-160: Our experiments across BUSI, Kvasir-Seg and Glas datasets validate our hypothesis, demonstrating better IoU (Intersection over Union) and F1 (Dice) scores on comparison with the SOTA methods.
elements.sent-2411-161: Furthermore, ablation studies show the key role of each architectural component, particularly highlighting how the bag of activation functions contributes to the overall performance enhancements.
elements.sent-2411-162: In summary, KAN-Mamba FusionNet advances accuracy and robustness of medical image segmentation and is efficient in terms of computation.
elements.sent-2411-163: The use of bag-of-activation functions offers a flexible approach to augmenting neural netowrk efficacy, tackling intricate visual data issues in healthcare, and perhaps improved patient outcomes via more accurate segmentation masks.
elements.sent-2411-164: By adaptively integrating various activation functions, BoA improves the versatility and robustness of neural networks in diverse ML applications.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-165: After receiving paper reviews, authors may optionally submit a rebuttal to address the reviewers' comments, which will be limited to a {\bf one page PDF file.}
elements.sent-2411-166: Please follow the steps and style guidelines outlined below for submitting your author response.
elements.sent-2411-167: The author rebuttal is optional and, following similar guidelines to previous conferences, is meant to provide you with an opportunity to rebut factual errors or to supply additional information requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-168: It is NOT intended to add new contributions (theorems, algorithms, experiments) that were absent in the original submission and NOT specifically requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-169: You may optionally add a figure, graph, or proof to your rebuttal to better illustrate your answer to the reviewers' comments.
elements.sent-2411-170: Per a passed 2018 PAMI-TC motion, reviewers should refrain from requesting significant additional experiments for the rebuttal or penalize for lack of additional experiments.
elements.sent-2411-171: Authors should refrain from including new experimental results in the rebuttal, especially when not specifically requested to do so by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-172: Authors may include figures with illustrations or comparison tables of results reported in the submission/supplemental material or in other papers.
elements.sent-2411-173: Just like the original submission, the rebuttal must maintain anonymity and cannot include external links that reveal the author identity or circumvent the length restriction.
elements.sent-2411-174: The rebuttal must comply with this template (the use of sections is not required, though it is recommended to structure the rebuttal for ease of reading).
elements.sent-2411-175: Author responses must be no longer than 1 page in length including any references and figures.
elements.sent-2411-176: Overlength responses will simply not be reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-177: This includes responses where the margins and formatting are deemed to have been significantly altered from those laid down by this style guide.
elements.sent-2411-178: Note that this \LaTeX\ guide already sets figure captions and references in a smaller font.
elements.sent-2411-179: All text must be in a two-column format.
elements.sent-2411-180: The total allowable size of the text area is $6\frac78$ inches (17.46 cm) wide by $8\frac78$ inches (22.54 cm) high.
elements.sent-2411-181: Columns are to be $3\frac14$ inches (8.25 cm) wide, with a $\frac{5 {16}$ inch (0.8 cm) space between them.}
elements.sent-2411-182: The top margin should begin 1 inch (2.54 cm) from the top edge of the page.
elements.sent-2411-183: The bottom margin should be $1\frac{1 {8}$ inches (2.86 cm) from the bottom edge of the page for $8.5 \times 11$-inch paper; for A4 paper, approximately $1\frac{5 {8}$ inches (4.13 cm) from the bottom edge of the page.}}
elements.sent-2411-185: It is important for readers to be able to refer to any particular equation.
elements.sent-2411-186: Wherever Times is specified, Times Roman may also be used.
elements.sent-2411-187: Main text should be in 10-point Times, single-spaced.
elements.sent-2411-188: Section headings should be in 10 or 12 point Times.
elements.sent-2411-189: All paragraphs should be indented 1 pica (approx.~$\frac{1 {6}$ inch or 0.422 cm).}
elements.sent-2411-190: Figure and table captions should be 9-point Roman type as in \cref{fig:onecol .}
elements.sent-2411-191: List and number all bibliographical references in 9-point Times, single-spaced, at the end of your response.
elements.sent-2411-192: When referenced in the text, enclose the citation number in square brackets, for example~\cite{Alpher05 .}
elements.sent-2411-193: Where appropriate, include the name(s) of editors of referenced books.
elements.figure-2411-010: \fbox{\rule{0pt}{0.5in} \rule{0.9\linewidth}{0pt}} \caption{Example of caption. It is set in Roman so that mathematics (always set in Roman: $B \sin A = A \sin B$) may be included without an ugly clash.} \label{fig:onecol}
elements.sent-2411-194: To avoid ambiguities, it is best if the numbering for equations, figures, tables, and references in the author response does not overlap with that in the main paper (the reviewer may wonder if you talk about \cref{fig:onecol in the author response or in the paper).}
elements.sent-2411-197: Please ensure that any point you wish to make is resolvable in a printed copy of the response.
elements.sent-2411-198: Resize fonts in figures to match the font in the body text, and choose line widths which render effectively in print.
elements.sent-2411-199: Readers (and reviewers), even of an electronic copy, may choose to print your response in order to read it.
elements.sent-2411-200: You cannot insist that they do otherwise, and therefore must not assume that they can zoom in to see tiny details on a graphic.
elements.sent-2411-201: When placing figures in \LaTeX, it is almost always best to use \verb+\includegraphics+, and to specify the figure width as a multiple of the line width as in the example below { .}
elements.sent-2411-202: Medical image segmentation is essential for applications like robotic surgeries, disease diagnosis, and treatment planning.
elements.sent-2411-203: Recently, various deep-learning models have been proposed to enhance medical image segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-204: One promising approach utilizes Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs), which better capture non-linearity in input data.
elements.sent-2411-205: However, they are unable to effectively capture long-range dependencies, which are required to accurately segment complex medical images and, by that, improve diagnostic accuracy in clinical settings.
elements.sent-2411-206: Neural networks such as Mamba can handle long-range dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-207: However, they have a limited ability to accurately capture non-linearities in the images as compared to KANs.
elements.sent-2411-208: Thus, we propose a novel architecture, the KAN-Mamba FusionNet, which improves segmentation accuracy by effectively capturing the non-linearities from input and handling long-range dependencies with the newly proposed KAMBA block.
elements.sent-2411-209: We evaluated the proposed KAN-Mamba FusionNet on three distinct medical image segmentation datasets: BUSI, Kvasir-Seg, and GlaS - and found it consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in IoU and F1 scores.
elements.sent-2411-210: Further, we examined the effects of various components and assessed their contributions to the overall model performance via ablation studies.
elements.sent-2411-211: The findings highlight the effectiveness of this methodology for reliable medical image segmentation, providing a unique approach to address intricate visual data issues in healthcare.
elements.sent-2411-212: Techniques like Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are popularly used to mark individual pixels for segmentation tasks to identify tumor locations, organs, and relevant anatomical features \cite{church2019deep,kevric2022deep,zhang2021medical,yamashita2018cnn .}
elements.sent-2411-213: Classical CNN-based approaches like UNet models \cite{ronneberger2015unet use the encoder and decoder architecture for image segmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-214: The UNet variations, UNet \cite{ronneberger2015unet , UNet++ \cite{zhou2018unetplusplus , and UNet3+ \cite{huang2020unet3plus use hierarchical techniques to extract features from the input image.}}}
elements.sent-2411-215: Thus, they fail to get global contextual dependencies, which are essential to segment varied-sized anatomical parts from the medical image.
elements.sent-2411-216: Unlike CNNs, ViT, i.e., vision transformers \cite{dosovitskiy2021image based models like Medical Transformer \cite{valanarasu2021medical and TransUNet \cite{chen2021transunet use attention mechanism to capture the global contextual dependencies and can accept varied input sizes, making them better suitable for medical image segmentation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-217: In order to overcome the challenges of the higher computational complexity of transformers, state space models (SSM) using linear RNNs (Recurrent Neural Networks) are proposed in Mamba architecture and its variations \cite{gu2023mamba,ma2024umamba,wang2024segmamba,wang2024visionmamba .}
elements.sent-2411-218: These models also address the limitations of CNNs in capturing long-range dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-219: However, these models have conventional convolution layers, which use static activation functions and thereby, limiting their ability to handle non-linearities.
elements.sent-2411-220: Recent architectures like U-KAN \cite{li2024ukan , employs KAN layers to better capture the non-linear intricacies in the images.}
elements.sent-2411-221: The KAN layers use learnable activation functions, to efficiently approximate multivariate continuous functions using univariate transformations, enabling the capture of non-linearities in the input data \cite{liu2024kan .}
elements.sent-2411-222: However these architectures cannot capture long-range dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-223: Further, neither U-KAN nor Mamba considers specific attention to local features, which are required to extract fine-grained features to identify the boundaries of irregular shapes and sizes of the anatomical structures.
elements.sent-2411-224: In this paper, we propose a novel architecture, KAN-Mamba FusionNet, to accurately segment medical images by handling long-range dependencies and efficiently capturing non-linearities.
elements.sent-2411-225: As part of this architecture, we designed a novel KAMBA block by utilizing State Space Model (SSM), KAN layer, spatial attention module, bag of activation (BoA) functions and a skip connection.
elements.sent-2411-226: Our proposed architecture outperforms the state-of-the-art methods on distinct medical image datasets.
elements.sent-2411-227: The contributions are summarised as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-228: We propose a novel KAMBA block to handle long-range dependencies, while efficiently capturing non-linearities and attention to relevant local features.
elements.sent-2411-229: We also propose bag of activation (BoA) functions to better capture non-linearities in the KAMBA framework against the use of a pre-defined single activation function in various connections.
elements.sent-2411-230: We present a KAN-Mamba FusionNet architecture by incorporating KAMBA block in the U-KAN pipeline to improve performance of medical image segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-231: We evaluated our proposed architecture on medical image datasets, BUSI \cite{al2020busi , GlaS \cite{valanarasu2020kiu and Kvasir-Seg \cite{jha2020kvasir and provided ablation study in the end.}}}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{Architecture.drawio.png} \caption{An overview of the proposed KAN-Mamba FusionNet Architecture with the detailed framework of KAMBA block.} \label{fig:overall-image}
elements.sent-2411-232: Fig. \ref{fig:overall-image illustrates the proposed KAN-Mamba FusionNet architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-233: It consists of three main components: 1) KAN Block, 2) KAMBA Block, and 3) Convolution Block.
elements.sent-2411-234: The proposed architecture incorporates KAMBA block in the U-KAN \cite{liu2024kan pipeline, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:overall-image .}}
elements.sent-2411-235: The loss for overall architecture is calculated using a combination of binary cross entropy (BCE) loss function and dice loss function as explained in detail in section \ref{sec:implementation details .}
elements.sent-2411-236: The implementation of the architecture blocks is explained in detail as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-237: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) offer an innovative alternative to traditional MLPs for medical segmentation \cite{li2024ukan,liu2024kan .}
elements.sent-2411-238: KANs efficiently approximate multivariate continuous functions using univariate transformations, enabling the capture of non-linearities in high-dimensional data.
elements.sent-2411-239: KAN layers have demonstrated to better capture non-linearities from the input data by using learnable activation functions on the weight edges connecting two layers of the neural network.
elements.sent-2411-240: This makes the neural network more adaptive to the various intricacies of the data, helping in capturing more complex and non-linear patterns.
elements.sent-2411-241: The KAN having $K$ layers is formulated as: \begin{equation}\text{KAN}(Z) = (\Phi_{K-1} \circ \Phi_{K-2} \circ \cdots \circ \Phi_1 \circ \Phi_0) Z\end{equation} where, $\Phi$ are expressed using learnable activation functions $\phi$, such that, \begin{equation}\Phi = \{\phi_{q,p}\}, \quad p = 1, 2, \dots, n_{\text{in}}, \quad q = 1, 2, \dots, n_{\text{out}}\end{equation} KANs demonstrate promising accuracy and interpretability over other neural networks \cite{liu2024kan .}
elements.sent-2411-242: Taking advantage of these properties offered by KAN layers, we employ KAN blocks in our architecture.
elements.sent-2411-243: As shown in KAN block in Fig. \ref{fig:overall-image , the input features are passed through a unit of the KAN layer followed by a depthwise convolution $DwConv$ layer.}
elements.sent-2411-244: As illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:overall-image , KAMBA block consists of a KAN block, BoA, State Space Model (SSM), a spatial attention module and a skip connection.}
elements.sent-2411-245: These modules help KAMBA block in ensuring long-range dependencies, extracting fine-grained features, while better handling non-linear patterns in the input images.
elements.sent-2411-246: Following subsections explain each module in detail to address how these properties are achieved.
elements.sent-2411-247: KAN Block As explained in section \ref{KAN , the KAN block ensures capturing non-linearities in the input data through learnable activation functions.}
elements.sent-2411-248: Bag of Activation Functions Different activation functions capture distinct aspects of data, each bringing their own strengths and weaknesses to a neural network.
elements.sent-2411-249: For example, ReLU introduces sparsity by activating only for positive inputs, which can lead to faster convergence and reduced computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-250: However, ReLU may face the ``dying ReLU'' problem, where neurons may stop learning or become inactive for consistent zero output.
elements.sent-2411-251: On the other hand, the sigmoid function is adept at modeling probabilities and is useful in scenarios requiring output values between 0 and 1.
elements.sent-2411-252: Nonetheless, it is prone to vanishing gradients, which may hamper the training of deep networks.
elements.sent-2411-253: Similarly, the hyperbolic tangent (tanh) activation function captures symmetric relationships around zero, providing outputs in the range of -1 to 1, which may be useful in centering the data and improving convergence.
elements.sent-2411-254: Yet, it also suffers from vanishing gradient issues \cite{lecun1998efficient .}
elements.sent-2411-255: To overcome these limitations, we propose bag of activation functions (BoA) in our KAMBA block, to replace a single pre-defined activation function in various connections, with a limited set of parameters.
elements.sent-2411-256: BoA is a learnable weighted composition of pre-defined activation functions.
elements.sent-2411-257: Mathematically, BoA can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\Psi(Z) = \sum_{p=1}^P \alpha_{p} \, \psi_{p}(Z), \quad where \sum_{p=1}^P \alpha_{p} = 1 \label{eq:weight_constraint}\end{equation} where $P$ is the number of activation functions used, $\alpha_{p}$ forms the learnable weight associated with activation function $\psi_{p}$.
elements.sent-2411-258: In our BoA layer, we have included ReLU, tanh, Softplus, GELU and SiLU activation functions while keeping the same initial weights.
elements.sent-2411-259: By learning and optimizing the weights associated with each activation function during training, the model can leverage the strengths of each activation function while mitigating their individual weaknesses.
elements.sent-2411-260: State Space Model (SSM) Recent researchers have emphasized the benefits of State Space Models (SSMs) \cite{amo2024,gu2023mamba,ma2024umamba in addressing the challenge of capturing long-range dependencies, which is crucial for long-sequence based tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-261: These models can be viewed as built on a combination of recurrent neural networks (RNNs) and convolutional neural networks (CNNs).
elements.sent-2411-262: These studies have demonstrated that SSMs model the evolution of hidden states through a set of linear recurrence relations for capturing long-range dependencies in input sequences.
elements.sent-2411-263: Moreover, these models are highly efficient at modeling dependencies for the larger segments of the input sequences due to their linear computational complexity in both time and space \cite{gu2023mamba .}
elements.sent-2411-264: Thus, modeling these long-range dependencies can effectively capture global spatial context that can help to accurately segment the relevant features in the input data.
elements.sent-2411-265: As a result, integrating SSM into the KAMBA block helps in efficiently improving the model's segmentation accuracy by considering the long-term relationships within the data.
elements.sent-2411-266: Spatial Attention Module The spatial attention module uses inter-spatial relationships among pixels to extract the fine-grained features to efficiently capture the boundaries of the irregular shapes from the input image.
elements.sent-2411-267: Mathematically, spatial attention over an intermediate feature map F can be expressed as \cite{woo2018cbam : \begin{equation}M_s(F) = \sigma\left(f_{7 \times 7}\left(\left[AvgPool(F); MaxPool(F)\right]\right)\right)\end{equation} \begin{equation}= \sigma\left(f_{7 \times 7}\left(\left[F_{s\text{avg}}; F_{s\text{max}}\right]\right)\right) \label{eq:spatial}\end{equation} here, $\sigma$ represents the sigmoid function acting over a larger convolution function $f_{7 \times 7}$ with filter size of 7 × 7 to capture more spatial features.}
elements.sent-2411-268: Skip Connection It has been added to avoid the vanishing gradient issues and improve feature extraction by skipping one or more layers from the neural network.
elements.sent-2411-269: Working of KAMBA Block The KAMBA block is added after the first convolution block in the KAN-Mamba FusionNet architecture, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:overall-image .}
elements.sent-2411-270: We positioned the KAMBA block after the first convolution layer to effectively capture the low-level features.
elements.sent-2411-271: This ensures the finer details of the input image are preserved early in the process.
elements.sent-2411-272: In KAMBA block, the input feature map ($Z_{in}$) passes through a KAN block ($KANB$).
elements.sent-2411-273: This is further processed by a layer of BoA ($\Psi$), as expressed in equation \eqref{eq:weight_constraint , followed by an SSM layer and a spatial attention module such that: \begin{equation}\quad Z_{\text{out}} = KANB(Z_{\text{in}}), \quad Z_{\text{out}}' = \Psi(Z_{\text{out}}), \quad Z_{\text{out}}'' = M_s(SSM(Z_{\text{out}}')), \quad\end{equation} where $M_s(.)$ represents the attention function as explained in equation \eqref{eq:spatial .}}
elements.sent-2411-274: The result of the above formulation is then combined with the output obtained from the parallel BoA layer as well as the initial input fed into the KAMBA block using a skip connection.
elements.sent-2411-275: Mathematically, the final output obtained through KAMBA block can be expressed as: \begin{equation}KAMBA(Z_\text{in}) = Z_{\text{out}}'' \oplus \Psi(Z_{\text{in}}) \oplus Z_{in}\end{equation} Thus, our proposed design of the KAMBA block facilitates the integration of SSM, spatial attention and KAN layers to improve the contextual understanding of the relevant anatomical structures in the input data.
elements.sent-2411-276: The Convolution block consists of 2D convolution layers, batch normalization layers, a max pooling layer and ReLU activation functions.
elements.sent-2411-277: These modalities help in feature extraction from the input data.
elements.sent-2411-278: We evaluated our model on three distinct publically available medical image segmentation datasets: Breast UltraSound Images (BUSI) \cite{al2020busi , Segmented Polyp Images (Kvasir-Seg) \cite{jha2020kvasir and Gland Segmentation Images (GlaS) \cite{valanarasu2020kiu .}}}
elements.sent-2411-279: These datasets contain input images as well as the corresponding ground truth masks, which are manually annotated by the medical experts and serve as reference standards for evaluating model performance.
elements.sent-2411-280: BUSI \cite{al2020busi : The dataset consists of ultrasound scans, along with their corresponding segmentation masks for identifying breast cancer-related tumors.}
elements.sent-2411-281: Kvasir-Seg \cite{jha2020kvasir : The dataset consists of 1000 gastrointestinal polyp images (polyps are precursors to colorectal cancer) and their corresponding segmentation masks.}
elements.sent-2411-282: GlaS \cite{valanarasu2020kiu : The dataset consists of gland segmentation images and is associated with the Hospital Clinic in Barcelona, Spain.}
elements.sent-2411-283: The model was implemented using PyTorch on an NVIDIA RTX A6000 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-284: The loss in KAN-Mamba FusionNet architecture is computed using a combination of binary cross entropy (BCE) loss and dice loss functions, measuring the deviation from ground truth labels for entire set of N pixels.
elements.sent-2411-285: These losses are defined in \eqref{bce and \eqref{dice as follows: \begin{equation}\text{Loss}_{\text{BCE}}(\hat{z},z) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( z_i \cdot \log(\hat{z}_i) + (1 - z_i) \cdot \log(1 - \hat{z}_i) \right) \label{bce}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\text{Loss}_{\text{Dice}}(\hat{z},z) = 1- \frac{2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} \hat{z}_i \cdot z_i + \text{c}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \hat{z}_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i + \text{c}} \label{dice}\end{equation} where $z_i$ and $\hat{z _i$ represent the pixel-wise label from actual and estimated measures respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-286: The BCE loss quantifies the pixel-wise classification error, whereas the dice loss is useful for obtaining accurate segmentation results, since it emphasizes region-based optimization.
elements.sent-2411-287: The dice loss also handles class imbalance in datasets by measuring the overlap between estimated and ground-truth masks.
elements.sent-2411-288: The combination of these two losses, as defined in equation \eqref{eq:loss , helps in faster and more consistent convergence, leading to more accurate clinically relevant segmentation results. \begin{equation}\text{Loss}_{\text{Final}} = 0.5*\text{Loss}_{\text{BCE}} + \text{Loss}_{\text{Dice}} \label{eq:loss}\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-289: To ensure fairness and reliability, all performance comparisons with SOTA models were conducted on the same GPU, NVIDIA RTX A6000, with three independent runs.
elements.sent-2411-290: The reported results are averages from the three independent runs.
elements.sent-2411-291: Additionally, we performed ablation studies of our model to study the effect of our proposed methods.
elements.figure-2411-012: \fontsize{7.8}{12}\selectfont \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \multirow{2}{*}{Method} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{BUSI \cite{al2020busi}} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{Kvasir-Seg \cite{jha2020kvasir}} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{GlaS \cite{valanarasu2020kiu}} IoU↑ F1↑ IoU↑ F1↑ IoU↑ F1↑ U-Net \cite{ronneberger2015unet} 57.63$\pm$0.43 72.21$\pm$0.11 72.41$\pm$0.37 83.43$\pm$1.12 82.58$\pm$0.63 90.47$\pm$0.45 U-NeXt \cite{valanarasu2022unext} 59.71$\pm$0.31 73.41$\pm$0.34 69.36$\pm$0.26 81.50$\pm$0.14 82.74$\pm$0.45 90.72$\pm$0.32 Rolling-UNet \cite{liu2024rolling} 61.57$\pm$0.27 74.51$\pm$0.04 75.51$\pm$1.07 85.71$\pm$0.42 84.21$\pm$0.74 91.43$\pm$0.78 U-Mamba \cite{ma2024umamba} 61.75$\pm$0.52 74.91$\pm$0.18 73.91$\pm$0.61 85.23$\pm$0.52 85.12$\pm$0.51 91.57$\pm$0.38 Seg. U-KAN \cite{li2024ukan} 63.64$\pm$0.72 76.68$\pm$0.79 75.85$\pm$0.53 85.96$\pm$0.35 85.45$\pm$0.34 91.95$\pm$0.31 \rowcolor[rgb]{0.678,0.847,0.902} 2cm{ KAN-Mamba FusionNet} 65.84$\pm$0.54 79.17$\pm$0.45 76.95$\pm$0.68 86.66$\pm$0.43 85.73$\pm$0.28 92.29$\pm$0.23 \end{tabular} \caption{Performance comparison of various segmentation methods on BUSI, Kvasir-Seg and GlaS datasets.} \label{tab:comparisons}
elements.sent-2411-292: We introduce comparisons to the five state-of-art methods, U-Net \cite{ronneberger2015unet , U-NeXt \cite{valanarasu2022unext , Rolling-UNet \cite{liu2024rolling , U-Mamba \cite{ma2024umamba , and Seg.. U-KAN \cite{li2024ukan .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-293: Our model performance, as demonstrated in terms of IoU and F1 score in Table \ref{tab:comparisons , clearly indicates its robustness and versatility in segmenting various datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-294: A consistent high F1 score across datasets indicates the effectiveness of the proposed KAN-Mamba FusionNet to identify true positives while minimizing the number of false positives and false negatives to avoid misdiagnosis.
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{pic.png} \caption{Visualized segmentation results on three distinct datasets: BUSI, Kvasir-Seg, and GlaS. For each dataset, an input image, the corresponding ground truth mask, and the outputs from SOTA methods and the proposed KAN-Mamba FusionNet model are shown.} \label{fig:visualization-image}
elements.sent-2411-295: Fig. \ref{fig:visualization-image shows the visualization of segmentation results of the proposed KAN-Mamba FusionNet model and SOTA methods on BUSI, Kvasir-Seg and GlaS datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-296: The visual outputs highlight that the proposed KAN-Mamba FusionNet model can effectively segment relevant anatomical features for different datasets.
elements.sent-2411-297: The proposed model performs better compared to the SOTA methods in accurately capturing the relevant features, including the boundaries of irregular shapes and sizes in the input images.
elements.sent-2411-298: Also, as can be inferred from these visualized segmentation results, the proposed model exhibits significantly fewer false positive regions compared to the SOTA methods.
elements.sent-2411-299: The closeness of the segmented results produced by the proposed method to the ground truth masks on different datasets strongly suggests the versatility and effectiveness of our model.
elements.sent-2411-300: It is also validated with the consistent high F1 score and IoU as shown in Table \ref{tab:comparisons .}
elements.sent-2411-301: Additionally, as shown in Table \ref{tab:complexity , we have calculated the GFLOPs and model parameters to emphasize that our model can be implemented with minimal trade-off in computational cost and performance.}

U-KAN \cite{li2024ukan} 13.87 6.36 \rowcolor[rgb]{0.678,0.847,0.902} KAN-Mamba FusionNet 13.96 6.37 \end{tabular} \caption{Model complexity comparison of various methods.} \label{tab:complexity}
elements.figure-2411-015: \fontsize{8}{12}\selectfont \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \multirow{2}{*}{Blocks and/or Layers} \multicolumn{6}{c|}{Performance Metrics} IoU$\uparrow$ F1$\uparrow$ Accuracy$\uparrow$ AUC$\uparrow$ Precision$\uparrow$ Recall$\uparrow$ Classical Mamba 60.87 75.12 92.63 91.56 73.93 72.74 Classical Mamba + KAN 62.73 77.43 93.43 92.04 74.14 75.22 Classical Mamba with BoA + KAN 64.45 77.32 93.12 93.48 76.65 74.48 \rowcolor[rgb]{0.678,0.847,0.902} KAMBA 65.84 79.17 94.18 94.27 77.78 79.64 \end{tabular} \caption{Ablation studies on the effect of introducing changes in the classical Mamba block on the model on BUSI dataset.} \label{tab:mamba}
elements.sent-2411-302: We comprehensively evaluated our proposed KAN-Mamba FusionNet across various settings, to thoroughly examine and investigate the impact, effectiveness and potential of the proposed methods.
elements.sent-2411-303: Effect of KAMBA Block: Table \ref{tab:mamba shows the results of the ablation study to validate the effect of adding KAMBA block.}
elements.sent-2411-304: We first evaluated classical Mamba with U-Net architecture.
elements.sent-2411-305: Then we replaced convolutional layers from classical Mamba with KAN.
elements.sent-2411-306: For the next experiment, we added BoA with the classical Mamba and KAN block.
elements.sent-2411-307: With each module addition, the table shows improved performance.
elements.sent-2411-308: Finally, our proposed KAMBA, which consists of SSM, KAN, BoA, spatial attention, and a skip connection, outperformed these models on the listed performance parameters.
elements.sent-2411-309: Effect of Bag of Activation Functions: Table \ref{tab:ablation1 shows the effect of adding BoA in the KAMBA block in our proposed KAN-Mamba FusionNet model, compared to using no activation function or a single activation function.}
elements.sent-2411-310: Introducing a single activation function (ReLU) results in improved performance of the model compared to using no activation function in the KAMBA block.
elements.sent-2411-311: Further, by incorporating a composition of multiple pre-defined activation functions, essentially a bag of activation (BoA) functions, the model's learning capability is further enhanced, leading to even better results.
elements.sent-2411-312: These results underscore the importance of adding BoA, demonstrating that the diversity of activation functions plays an important role in enhancing the performance of the model.
elements.figure-2411-016: \fontsize{8}{12}\selectfont \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \multirow{2}{*}{2cm{ Activation Functions}} \multicolumn{6}{c|}{Performance Metrics} IoU$\uparrow$ F1$\uparrow$ Accuracy$\uparrow$ AUC$\uparrow$ Precision$\uparrow$ Recall$\uparrow$ None 62.73 77.43 93.43 92.04 74.14 75.22 Single (ReLU) 64.61 78.26 93.91 92.12 77.59 74.87 \rowcolor[rgb]{0.678,0.847,0.902} Proposed BoA 65.84 79.17 94.18 94.27 77.78 79.64 \end{tabular} \caption{Ablation studies on the effect of bag of activation functions in KAMBA block of the model on BUSI dataset.} \label{tab:ablation1}
elements.sent-2411-313: In this paper, we propose a novel KAN-Mamba FusionNet architecture to improve the medical image segmentation accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-314: As part of this architecture, we have designed KAMBA block with KAN, BoA, SSM, spatial attention module and a skip connection to better capture non-linearities and handle long-range dependencies in the input data.
elements.sent-2411-315: Our experiments across the BUSI, Kvasir-Seg, and GlaS datasets validate our hypothesis, demonstrating better IoU and F1 scores in comparison with the state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-316: Furthermore, our ablation studies provide crucial insights, revealing the key role of each architectural component in the KAN-Mamba FusionNet model.
elements.sent-2411-317: In summary, our proposed approach considerably advances the overall accuracy, effectiveness and robustness of medical image segmentation with efficient computation.
